title: Web Fuzzing
html:
Web Fuzzing

What is Web Fuzzing?

Web fuzzing is a technique used to discover vulnerabilities, hidden resources, and security issues in web applications by automatically injecting a large set of input data into the application and analyzing its response. The goal is to identify unexpected behaviors or errors that could indicate potential security weaknesses or misconfigurations. Fuzzing is commonly employed in security testing to find: - Hidden directories and files - Insecure APIs and endpoints - SQL injection points - Cross-site scripting (XSS) vulnerabilities - Command injection flaws

Comparison: Brute-Forcing vs. Fuzzing

Brute-Forcing

Criteria

Systematically trying all possible combinations of input data to guess a specific value.

Definition

Crack passwords, keys, or other access credentials.

Purpose

Exhaustive search over all possible input combinations.

Methodology

Specific input or data, such as passwords or API keys.

Focus

Time-consuming due to exhaustive nature; less efficient for large input spaces.

Efficiency

Password crackers, key recovery tools.

Tools Used

Successful match of the correct input value.

Output

Miscellaneous Commands

Below are some useful commands that can aid in various tasks related to web fuzzing and testing.

Description

Command

Add a DNS entry for a specific IP address to the /etc/hosts file. This helps resolve domain names locally.

sudo sh -c 'echo "SERVER_IP academy.htb" >> /etc/hosts'

Create a sequence wordlist from 1 to 1000. Useful for brute-forcing numerical IDs or similar patterns.

for i in $(seq 1 1000); do echo $i >> ids.txt; done

Use curl to send a POST request with specific data and headers, simulating form submissions or API calls.

curl http://admin.academy.htb:PORT/admin/admin.php -X POST -d 'id=key' -H 'Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded'

Commonly Used SecLists Wordlists

[SecLists](https://github.com/danielmiessler/SecLists) is a collection of multiple types of wordlists used by security researchers and penetration testers. Below is a table of some commonly used wordlists from SecLists, which can be incredibly valuable during web fuzzing.

Description

Wordlist

General-Purpose Wordlist: Contains a broad range of common directory and file names on web servers. It's an excellent starting point for fuzzing and often yields valuable results.

/usr/share/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/common.txt

Directory-Focused Wordlist: A more extensive wordlist specifically focused on directory names. It's a good choice when you need a deeper dive into potential directories.

/usr/share/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/directory-list-2.3-medium.txt

Large Directory Wordlist: Boasts a massive collection of directory names compiled from various sources. It's a valuable resource for thorough fuzzing campaigns.

/usr/share/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/raft-large-directories.txt

Comprehensive Wordlist: A massive wordlist containing both directory and file names. Useful when you want to cast a wide net and explore all possibilities.

/usr/share/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/big.txt

Tips for Using Wordlists Effectively

Explanation

Tip

Select wordlists relevant to the target environment and technology stack for better results.

Choose the Right Wordlist

Use multiple wordlists together to increase the breadth of your fuzzing efforts.

Combine Wordlists

Modify existing wordlists or create your own based on specific knowledge about the target.

Customize Wordlists

Large wordlists can be resource-intensive; monitor performance and adjust as needed.

Monitor Performance

Utilize community-maintained wordlists for the latest and most effective fuzzing strategies.

Leverage Community Resources

Tools for Web Fuzzing

ffuf (Fuzz Faster U Fool)

ffuf is a fast web fuzzer written in Go that allows you to discover directories and files on web servers.

Description

Command

Basic fuzzing of a URL path.

ffuf -u http://example.com/FUZZ

Fuzz with a specific wordlist.

ffuf -u http://example.com/FUZZ -w wordlist.txt

Fuzz with a specific wordlist, automatically ignoring any comments in the wordlist.

ffuf -u http://example.com/FUZZ -w wordlist.txt -ic

Colorize the output for better readability.

ffuf -u http://example.com/FUZZ -w wordlist.txt -c

Filter results by status code (e.g., 200).

ffuf -u http://example.com/FUZZ -w wordlist.txt -mc 200

Filter results by matching a regex pattern.

ffuf -u http://example.com/FUZZ -w wordlist.txt -mr "Welcome"

Add extensions to each wordlist entry.

ffuf -u http://example.com/FUZZ -w wordlist.txt -e .php,.html

Set the number of threads (e.g., 50) for faster fuzzing.

ffuf -u http://example.com/FUZZ -w wordlist.txt -t 50

Use a proxy for requests.

ffuf -u http://example.com/FUZZ -w wordlist.txt -x http://127.0.0.1:8080

gobuster

gobuster is a tool used to brute-force URIs (directories and files) in web sites and DNS subdomains.

Description

Command

Directory fuzzing using a wordlist.

gobuster dir -u http://example.com -w wordlist.txt

Fuzz with specific extensions.

gobuster dir -u http://example.com -w wordlist.txt -x .php,.html

Filter results by status code (e.g., 200).

gobuster dir -u http://example.com -w wordlist.txt -s 200

Set the number of concurrent threads (e.g., 50).

gobuster dir -u http://example.com -w wordlist.txt -t 50

Output results to a file.

gobuster dir -u http://example.com -w wordlist.txt -o results.txt

Fuzz DNS subdomains using a wordlist.

gobuster dns -d example.com -w subdomains.txt

Show IP addresses of discovered subdomains.

gobuster dns -d example.com -w subdomains.txt -i

Silent mode; suppress output except for results.

gobuster dns -d example.com -w subdomains.txt -z

wneum (Wfuzz Fork)

wneum is a fork of wfuzz, a versatile web application fuzzer for testing web security.

Description

Command

Basic fuzzing excluding 404 responses.

wneum -c -z file,wordlist.txt --hc 404 http://example.com/FUZZ

Fuzz POST data in a form.

wneum -c -z file,wordlist.txt -d 'username=FUZZ&password=secret'

Use a specific cookie for requests.

wneum -c -z file,wordlist.txt -b 'session=12345'

Add a custom header to requests.

wneum -c -z file,wordlist.txt -H 'User-Agent: Wneum'

Set the number of threads (e.g., 50) for faster fuzzing.

wneum -c -z file,wordlist.txt -t 50

Fuzz using a specific HTTP method (e.g., PUT).

wneum -c -z file,wordlist.txt -u http://example.com/FUZZ -X PUT

Filter responses by content length (e.g., 50 bytes).

wneum -c -z file,wordlist.txt --hl 50

feroxbuster

feroxbuster is a tool designed for recursive content discovery and web fuzzing.

Description

Command

Basic URL fuzzing with a wordlist.

feroxbuster -u http://example.com -w wordlist.txt

Include specified file extensions in fuzzing.

feroxbuster -u http://example.com -w wordlist.txt -e

Exclude responses with status code 404.

feroxbuster -u http://example.com -w wordlist.txt -x 404

Set the number of concurrent threads (e.g., 50).

feroxbuster -u http://example.com -w wordlist.txt -t 50

Set maximum recursion depth (e.g., 3 levels deep).

feroxbuster -u http://example.com -w wordlist.txt --depth 3

Save output to a file.

feroxbuster -u http://example.com -w wordlist.txt -o results.txt

Disable recursion into discovered directories.

feroxbuster -u http://example.com -w wordlist.txt --no-recursion

Follow redirects automatically.

feroxbuster -u http://example.com -w wordlist.txt --url-redirect

Tips for Effective Web Fuzzing

Explanation

Tip

The quality of your wordlist can significantly impact results; choose or create wordlists relevant to the target.

Use Comprehensive Wordlists

Use status codes or response size filtering to focus on meaningful results and reduce noise.

Filter Unwanted Responses

Increase thread count for faster fuzzing, but be mindful of server capabilities to avoid overloading.

Adjust Thread Count

Pay attention to anomalies or unexpected behavior in server responses, indicating potential vulnerabilities.

Monitor Server Responses

Test different HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) to uncover potential vulnerabilities in all endpoints.

Fuzz with Various HTTP Methods

Web APIs: REST, SOAP, and GraphQL

What is a Web API?

A Web API (Application Programming Interface) is a set of rules and protocols for building and interacting with software applications. APIs allow different applications to communicate with each other over the internet, enabling the integration of various services and data exchange. Web APIs can be categorized into three main types: 1. REST (Representational State Transfer) 2. SOAP (Simple Object Access Protocol) 3. GraphQL Each type has its own unique characteristics, advantages, and use cases.

REST (Representational State Transfer)

REST is an architectural style that uses standard HTTP methods to access and manipulate resources on a server. It is known for its simplicity, scalability, and statelessness.

Description

Feature

Uses HTTP/HTTPS.

Protocol

Typically JSON, but can also use XML, HTML, or plain text.

Data Format

Each request from a client to a server must contain all the information needed.

Stateless

Uses HTTP methods: GET, POST, PUT, DELETE.

CRUD Operations

Highly scalable due to its stateless nature.

Scalability

Supports caching mechanisms to improve performance.

Caching

Uses endpoints that represent resources, e.g., /api/users/{id}.

URL Structure

Simplicity, flexibility, scalability.

Advantages

Can lead to over-fetching or under-fetching data.

Disadvantages

REST Fuzzing Tips:

Explanation

Tip

Ensure all CRUD operations are tested, as vulnerabilities might exist in any of them.

Test All HTTP Methods

Fuzz input fields with unexpected data types and formats to uncover validation issues.

Validate Input Fields

Analyze error messages for information disclosure or unintended behavior.

Examine Error Messages

Check for improper authentication and authorization controls.

Test Authentication Mechanisms

Test rate limits and throttling controls to ensure the API handles requests properly.

Explore API Rate Limits

Leverage a variety of payloads (SQLi, XSS) to test for potential security flaws.

Use Comprehensive Payloads

Test different resource representations (JSON, XML) for consistency and security flaws.

Check Resource Representation

SOAP (Simple Object Access Protocol)

SOAP is a protocol for exchanging structured information in web services. It uses XML as its message format and can operate over various protocols like HTTP, SMTP, or TCP.

Description

Feature

Protocol-independent but often used with HTTP/HTTPS.

Protocol

Exclusively XML.

Data Format

Can be either stateful or stateless.

Stateful/Stateless

Built-in security features for message integrity and confidentiality.

WS-Security

Uses specific error codes and messages.

Error Handling

More complex due to extensive standards and specifications.

Complexity

Highly extensible via WS-\* standards.

Extensibility

Strong security, reliability, and extensibility.

Advantages

More complex and less flexible compared to REST.

Disadvantages

SOAP Fuzzing Tips:

Explanation

Tip

Use WSDL (Web Services Description Language) files to understand the service's operations and inputs.

Analyze WSDL Files

Test XML inputs against the schema to identify validation flaws.

Validate XML Schema

Fuzz XML data to test for injection vulnerabilities.

Check for XML Injection

Fuzz SOAP headers to find potential security issues or misconfigurations.

Test SOAP Headers

Ensure security implementations are robust and correctly configured.

Evaluate WS-Security Implementations

Verify that transport-level security (e.g., HTTPS) is enforced and properly implemented.

Test Transport Security

Analyze SOAP fault messages for potential information leakage.

Examine SOAP Faults

GraphQL

GraphQL is a query language and runtime for APIs that allows clients to request specific data and define the structure of the response.

Description

Feature

Uses HTTP/HTTPS, typically over POST requests.

Protocol

JSON-based queries and responses.

Data Format

Stateless architecture.

Stateful/Stateless

Clients can request exactly what they need, minimizing over-fetching and under-fetching.

Query Flexibility

Typically uses a single endpoint for all operations.

Single Endpoint

Allows clients to query the API schema for available operations and data types.

Introspection

Efficiency, flexibility, and powerful developer tooling.

Advantages

Potential for complex queries leading to performance issues if not properly managed.

Disadvantages

GraphQL Fuzzing Tips:

Explanation

Tip

Evaluate the server's handling of deeply nested or complex queries to avoid performance bottlenecks.

Test Query Depth and Complexity

Fuzz input arguments with unexpected values and data types to uncover validation flaws.

Validate Input Types and Arguments

Test the server's response to aliased queries and batching for potential information leakage.

Examine Query Aliasing and Batching

Ensure introspection is not exposing sensitive information or internal schema details.

Check for Introspection Misuse

Verify that access controls are properly enforced for different queries and operations.

Assess Authorization Controls

Test rate limits to ensure the API can handle excessive or malicious requests appropriately.

Evaluate Rate Limiting

Mutations can alter data; test for security issues and improper input validation.

Fuzz Mutations
